"Han asked John, "What are your plans for the weekend?"

John replied, "I'm planning to explore Harvard University and learn more about it.  In fact, I intend to apply to Harvard after high school. It's best to get familiar with it beforehand."

Han responded, "Oh, wow! I didn't know you were religious. You seem more like a science enthusiast, like Leonard from The Big Bang Theory."

John chuckled, "Come on, Han, don't be dramatic. Many Americans, including scientists, believe in religion."

"But I thought science and religion were incompatible," Han stated, sounding surprised.

"They can be compatible," John explained. "The U.S. is both the most technologically advanced and the most religious country in the world."

"I've heard that," Han admitted, "but I don't understand. How can people believe in God when they know God doesn't exist?"

John replied, "That's a complex topic. You'll understand better when you learn more about U.S. history. Religion has always been a significant part of American life.  Even Harvard University, a highly academic institution, was, in a way, shaped by religion."

Han questioned, "Really?"

"Absolutely!" John affirmed. "It was founded by a group of Puritan immigrants from England."

"Why Puritans?" Han asked. "Where were the non-religious educators?"

"Most early English immigrants to the United States were Puritans," John stated.

"Why were they predominantly religious?" Han inquired.

John explained, "During the 16th and 17th centuries, there were religious reforms in England. Many religious dissenters faced persecution and fled to other countries."

"So, religious freedom wasn't a reality in England?" Han concluded.

"Exactly," John agreed. "That's why the Puritans moved to the New World, hoping to create a haven for Puritans. Later, these Puritans established Harvard College."

Han wondered, "Why did they want to found a college?"

John answered, "Many Puritans had received a classical education at Oxford and Cambridge Universities in England. They wanted their descendants to have the same opportunities."

Han nodded in understanding.

"Initially, Harvard was called 'New College' and didn't have a formal name," John added.

"When did it become Harvard University?" Han asked.

"In 1639, it was renamed after its primary benefactor, John Harvard," John explained. "Interestingly, John Harvard was a clergyman."

"It seems Harvard was destined to be influenced by religion," Han mused.

"It's not an exaggeration to say that without religion, there wouldn't be Harvard," John remarked.

Han exclaimed, "Wow, I never knew religion played such a crucial role in Harvard's history."

"Religion has been an indispensable part of Harvard's history, as well as the history of the United States," John affirmed.

Han admitted, "I'm starting to understand Harvard better. I realize my knowledge about it is limited. I have much work to do before applying."

John advised, "Remember, Harvard is part of the United States.  To truly understand Harvard, you need to grasp U.S. history and culture. They are closely intertwined."

Han agreed, "That makes sense. I have a lot to learn from you."
